title: Pakistan Ups the Ante on Indus Water Dispute With India
gist: Pakistan is escalating the Indus Waters dispute after India held the 1960 World Bank-brokered treaty 'in abeyance' following the Pahalgam attack, threatening a framework that for decades divided six rivers and managed a vital shared resource between the rivals.

insights.claim: The 1960 Indus Waters Treaty, brokered by the World Bank, allocated the western rivers Indus, Jhelum, and Chenab to Pakistan and the eastern rivers Ravi, Beas, and Sutlej to India.
insights.speaker: Umair Jamal
insights.so_what: Establishes the baseline whose breakdown would threaten Pakistan's agricultural water security and regional stability.

insights.claim: India was permitted only limited non-consumptive use of the western rivers (primarily hydropower), with the treaty explicitly prohibiting changes to flows that could undermine Pakistan's water access.
insights.speaker: Umair Jamal
insights.so_what: Defines the legal red lines whose breach is the core escalation risk in the dispute.
insights.locator: India was also permitted limited non-consumptive use

insights.claim: India held the Indus Waters Treaty in abeyance following a terrorist attack in Pahalgam, Indian-administered Kashmir, that killed 26 people, blaming Islamabad without presenting convincing evidence.
insights.speaker: Umair Jamal
insights.so_what: Weaponizing water rights marks a sharp escalation lever in India-Pakistan tensions with cross-border economic spillover.
insights.locator: Last year, however, India decided to hold the treaty in abeyance

insights.claim: Pakistan formally denied responsibility for the Pahalgam attack and called for a neutral, independent investigation as it ups the ante on the water dispute.
insights.speaker: Umair Jamal
insights.so_what: The dispute is hardening into a diplomatic standoff with no agreed adjudication path.
